title: Toward Charge-Dependent Tests of the Equivalence Principle: A Phenomenological Parameter and an Unexplored Frontier
abstract: We introduce and define the phenomenological parameter $κ$, defined by $Δa/g = κ\, Δ(q/m)$, to quantify potential linear coupling between electric charge and gravitational acceleration. A synthesis of existing precision equivalence principle experiments yields the first quantitative estimate of the effective sensitivity to this coupling: $|κ| < 2.1 \times 10^{-4}~\si{\kilo\gram\per\coulomb}$ at 95\% confidence level. This constraint is approximately eleven orders of magnitude less stringent than corresponding bounds on composition-dependent violations, revealing that the electromagnetic axis remains a largely underexplored frontier in empirical gravity. We connect $κ$ to established frameworks -- the Standard-Model Extension and the $THεμ$ formalism -- showing that it occupies a region of parameter space untouched by existing high-precision tests. An effective field theory analysis shows that dimension-six operators that couple curvature directly to the electromagnetic field strength are suppressed by the minuscule terrestrial spacetime curvature ($G_N ρ_\oplus \sim 10^{-55}~\text{GeV}^2$) and are therefore phenomenologically irrelevant. Consequently, a future measurement of $κ$ at an accessible level would not probe minimal geometric couplings but would signal physics beyond minimal gravitational EFT, such as mediation by light scalar fields as in Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton theory. We examine the Schiff-Barnhill effect, the primary systematic background for any such measurement, and show how it can be separated from a genuine signal. We outline the necessary experimental strategy, focused on maximizing charge-to-mass ratio differences, to transform this overlooked axis into a targeted probe for new physics.
summary: The paper introduces a new parameter, k, to quantify potential violations of the Equivalence Principle involving electric charge. It provides the first quantitative bound on this coupling and argues that future experiments maximizing charge-to-mass ratios could probe new physics like light scalar fields.
main_problem: Investigating potential violations of the Weak Equivalence Principle (WEP) specifically through a charge-dependent gravitational coupling, an 'electromagnetic axis' that remains largely underexplored.
main_result: Establishes a new phenomenological upper limit of |k| < 2.1 x 10^-4 kg/C at 95% confidence level and demonstrates that a detectable signal would likely indicate physics beyond minimal gravitational EFT, such as light scalar fields.
method: Performs a quantitative synthesis of existing precision equivalence principle experiments using Monte Carlo analysis to propagate uncertainties in acceleration sensitivity and charge-to-mass ratios.
model_or_system: The paper utilizes phenomenological frameworks including the Standard-Model Extension (SME), the THepsilonmu formalism, and Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton (EMD) theory, while evaluating experimental platforms like optically levitated nanoparticles and atom interferometry.
key_observables: The phenomenological parameter k (quantifying the linear coupling between electric charge and gravitational acceleration) and the differential acceleration delta a/g.
important_parameters: The coupling parameter k (kg/C), the charge-to-mass ratio difference delta(q/m), the dilaton coupling alpha, and the terrestrial curvature scale G_N rho_earth.
main_assumptions: Assumes the Earth acts as a source of the dilaton field and that the primary systematic background is the Schiff-Barnhill effect; the statistical result is subject to the accuracy of input experimental constraints.
figures_summary: The notes mention a probability density function (Fig. 1) showing the distribution of the upper limits on k.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the k parameter, quantifies the existing experimental gap, connects k to established theoretical frameworks (SME, EMD, EFT), performs a statistical synthesis of experimental data to derive a new bound, analyzes systematic backgrounds (Schiff-Barnhill), and proposes future experimental strategies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Probability distribution for the upper limit on |κ| derived from Monte Carlo analysis of current experimental constraints. The differential acceleration sensitivity σ∆a/g is modeled as 10−15 ± 20%, and the maximum differential charge-to-mass ratio ∆(q/m)max as 10−11 C kg−1 with a factor of 5 uncertainty. Vertical lines indicate the median (9.8 × 10−5 kg C−1, dashed red) and the 95% confidence level (2.1 × 10−4 kg C−1, solid orange).
